title: Using AI in Projects
text:
AI has many uses, from creating ideas, timelines, to-do lists, providing more defined questions, and breaking projects down into smaller steps. To start using AI, familiarity with AI models is crucial to understanding how AI can help you in your projects. Once comfortability with the AI model is established, you can use AI to help you brainstorm topics or refine research questions. Then, AI can help create a to-do list of the steps needed to produce the project. Next, AI could create a timeline of how long each step should take and how much time you should put into each section. This would give you a good estimate of the project length and how much effort is expected with each step. AI can even help you create the first rough draft of the project which helps ease the “blank page” anxiety most people have when first starting out. AI is incredibly helpful with creating rough drafts of assignments or projects but should never be used as the final draft. There are many steps we should use after using AI in the creation process to ensure originality and authenticity, including fact checking, editing, final draft creation without the use of AI, and AI documentation because not all the work was your own creation. AI is helpful with the brainstorming process and checking for mistakes but there is only so much AI can do, much of the work still falls on the individual to do and create.
The two AI models I will be using on my project are ChatGPT and Grok. AI can be leveraged to speed up learning by summarizing articles, simplifying complex information, and teaching you how to ask more specific questions to achieve the desired results. I will also use AI to build an outline and curate steps that best fit my workflow and the project I want to create.

title: Breiman and Shmueli
text:
Comparing “Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures” and “To Explain or to Predict?”
Statistical Modeling: The Two Cultures tackled the issue of data modeling being the primary approach in the science field, but algorithmic models are often more reliable for predictions in larger data sets and handling modern problems. For instance, Breiman discusses problems in data modeling by explaining a residual analysis where a data set is limited to two or three variables. He mentions that a goodness-of-fit test “lacks power in more than a few dimensions.” (203, Breiman) and how an acceptable plot does not mean that it is necessarily a good plot to use for data. He also explains that researchers are not usually looking for if the models they use fit the data, but if the model is new, clever, and sets them apart. Algorithmic models, although simpler, provide a higher predictive accuracy with more reliable information (214, Breiman), which is beneficial because loosely accurate results can lead to unanswered questions and questioning the research.
To Explain or to Predict? discusses the differences between explanatory modeling and predictive modeling and how combining the two can cause problems because of their differences. The distinction between the two is important because researchers assume that because a model explains well, it also predicts results well but that is not always true. Shmueli explains the differences, stating, “measurable data is not an accurate representation of their underlying constructs. The operationalization of theories… creates a disparity between the ability to explain at the conceptual level and the ability to generate predictions” (293, Shmueli). Because a model can explain the data and represent the research numerically and abstractly, does not mean the conclusions drawn from the explanatory models will represent the research with the most accuracy.
Similar to Breiman, good models and measurable data do not mean that they create the best predictions or the best results. Both articles challenge the idea of using complex language and models for better predictions. Breiman suggests that simpler models are more reliable and Shmueli agrees that “blending simpler models [provides] more accurate predictions” (302, Shmueli). Both articles reject the idea of over explaining concepts and using complex models for the sake of originality or sophistication. They also encourage other researchers to think more about the goals of their research and how their models and predictions fit with their research, instead of trying to have their research match the models. However, there are differences between the articles; Shmueli focuses on study design and how explanations and predictions are not related to understanding, while Breiman centers his article on the overuse of data models. Shmueli discusses why different goals matter for current and future research, whereas Breiman suggests that research practices should change and embrace algorithmic models as a reliable method of data visualization. Even though each author takes a different approach to predictions and data, they both criticize researchers who are stubborn with their approaches and who do not acknowledge future impacts of their studies.
Breiman, Leo. 2001. “Statistical modeling: The two cultures (with comments and a rejoinder by the author)”. Statistical Science, 16(3), pp.199-231
Shmueli, Galit. 2010. “To explain or to predict?.” Statistical science 25, no. 3: 289-310

title: AI and Originality
text:
With the emergence of AI, ChatGPT, and homework help, it has become easier to think less and have the computer work more. People are becoming more unoriginal and relying on AI and the internet to do the thinking for them. Previously when writing a research paper, a person would have to sift through articles, Google Scholar, and books to find evidence that supports their topic, but now, ChatGPT is able to populate 10 articles that directly correlate to the research in seconds. AI is decreasing our cognitive load and capabilities because it can give us precise information, ideas, answers, and work within no time. We no longer have to sit and think up research ideas; we can simply ask ChatGPT and it will have as many ideas as we want that all relate to our interests.
Our creative endeavors have also changed with AI. A major controversy with AI is its use in art. AI creates new ideas, paintings, music compositions, videos and stories and takes away from humans doing the thinking. We don’t have to put in the mental effort to think of a new story or a new project idea because AI can generate those same ideas in a fraction of the time, saving humans time and energy. Even though those creative projects still exist and there is a huge market for it, there are also many companies and people who prefer to use AI to get the same result with half the effort.
Ai is built with an algorithm that is honestly lackluster. It is generic and the “safe option”. AI won’t give you the out-of-the-box topics or ideas you’re searching for because it is trained to appeal to the masses. And people stop searching for those far fetched ideas because AI is also training us. When we are only given the safe option or the average response, we are becoming more accustomed to accepting mediocrity and not thinking outside of what we know. Often, the “good” ideas are found through trail and error or human connection and if we are not given the opportunities to fail or connect, we won’t achieve the same results. The “safe option” can also refer to word choices. There are countless times where I have googled synonyms for “discussed” and used the first synonym to pop up instead of using my own writing style and thinking about what I would want to use. We are becoming conditioned to ignore our own thoughts and go with the “safe option” we know will be more understood.
AGI stands for artificial general intelligence. It is an advanced form of AI that can process and learn at an above or equal level to human intelligence. Is it able to apply knowledge learned in one area to another unrelated area with ease. AGI affects scientific research because it will make it easier to connect different scientific disciplines and papers in ways that people would not normally think of. AGI will also be able to understand information, make correlations, understand causation, and design experiments to prove the theories without any effort or time. Effectively, AGI would be able to do the same thinking and designing that a human can do in no time and on a greater scale. If AGI were to be used in research, I believe it would negatively impact researchers, their jobs, and change the way research is conducted. The need for data analysts would decrease and researchers would no longer have to think up research ideas. Overall, AGI in research would drastically change the way research is conducted and change a researcher’s role to more of an overseer than a creator.

title: Assignment 1
text:
When working with the data set, I noticed that there were some missing data points; for instance, in highincome, votekmt_nm, and income. I also noticed that there are highly skewed variables like age. There is an overrepresented number of older adults compared to middle aged adults. There also seems to be duplicated results. There are many entries from different districts while some districts only have a couple entries. That may be because of the number of people in each district, or because of duplicated entries.
To deal with missing values, I used a code source to clean up the data and change every missing value which was represented by -9 or 99, to N/A. I did so by running a summary to see which variables were missing using colSums(is.na(teds_2016)). Then the dplyr package changed all the variables.
To compare different groups like Tondu, female, age and more we would first need to find out what each variable is. For example, female is binary because there is only the option of 1 or 2, male or female; this is the same for dpp, yes or no. Taiwanese and econ_worse are also binary. Other variables like age and income are continuous are number based. Edu is an ordinal variable because it can be ranked by education level. Next, the method used would be based on Tondu’s type. If Tondu was binary, I would use female and Tondu since they are both binary variables and run a bivariate analysis because there are only two variables with two options. A chi squared test would probably be the most useful for this variable interaction. To measure continuous and binary variables, like tondu and age, I would use a boxplot because you would be able to see how the age differs when Tondu is yes and when it is no. This is also a good way to show outliers because it would show up way outside of the boxed area. Since edu is an ordinal variable, a chi squared test would show if there’s an association or if the two variables are independent.
For votetsai, first we must identify what type of variable it is. It is a binary variable because it only has 1 or 0, did or did not vote for Tsai. A chi-squared test would be used to see if each variable is independent of each other.

title: Research
text:
Reading Notebook
My reading notebook starts by listing out the book’s information. This includes the title, genre, author, type of reading, and what I hope to accomplish by the end. Once finished, I also like to write a summary of the reading and rate it out of 5 stars on understandability, captivation, length to meaningful content ratio, and how effectively it helped me reach the reason why I read it to begin with. I also include key points, vocabulary I didn’t understand, and meaningful arguments that made me think. If I am reading a research article or an academic book, I will list out a question or two I still have about the reading to bring forth discussion and future research.
Reading Routine My reading routine varies depending on the type of reading and the length. If I am reading an academic article, I will read at my desk in the evening with my ceiling fan light on. This allows me to stay alert and really digest what I am reading. My desk also gives me access to my notes or paper to jot something down. If I am reading for pleasure, I will read in a lounge chair at any time of the day but particularly at night. I will have dim lights or a lamp nearby and I am usually with my dog and a blanket.
Reading Effectiveness Strategies I cannot read in total silence so I will sometimes turn on the tv to a channel I’m not interested in or open my window to listen to cars going by. I also turn my phone off for every material I read because my phone easily distracts me and I will lose focus. I will also look up other people’s understandings of readings to compare my own synopsis and make sure that I understand key details. I will go back and reread if I am missing something that other people are pointing out. Understanding what I am reading is the most important part of research and academic reviews. Rereading specific sections, summarizing after a few pages, breaking difficult concepts down into smaller parts, and asking questions are critical components of effective reading. One cannot simply read an article, lack understanding and say they have a grasp on the material and research being conducted.

title: Humans and Knowledge
text:
Humans and Knowledge
There are multiple ways that humans acquire knowledge, through experiencing the world around them, shared knowledge, self-reflection, reasoning, and gut instincts. Humans use their senses: sight, smell, touch, taste, and sound, to learn about their environment. We learn that ice is cold by tasting or feeling its cold. We touch a cactus and get pricked; we learn that you can’t grab a cactus with your bare hands because you will get injured. Shared knowledge is also a way that humans acquire knowledge. Stories passed down by generations have lost credibility since they are not from the source, but are still beneficial to understanding our history and sharing past experiences. We learn that certain berries are poisonous because people have died when consuming them. We learn of the hardships and tribulations people in history faced because of the stories of those who came before us.
Similarly, self-reflection allows us to gain knowledge. When we reflect, we look back on our past experiences and conclude the best outcomes and how we feel. Pattern recognition helps us find our triggers for certain emotions and how people will react if we act in a specific manner. Reasoning is another main type of knowledge acquisition. We use deduction and logical fallacies to determine what makes logical sense and what doesn’t. We know that we have a brain inside our heads, without most of us ever seeing our own, because every person who has died or been operated on has had a brain. Therefore, we can deduce that because everyone else has a brain, we do as well. Lastly, gut instinct lets us acquire knowledge. We don’t inherently know that a bear will kill us, but being chased and filled with adrenaline doesn’t allow us to stick around to find out. We run the risk that the bear might kill us. This also goes for suspicious situations. If there is a person who makes us feel uneasy, but there is no concrete evidence as to why, we put our guard up and take precautions anyway, or try to create as much distance as possible from the threat. Similarly, a pregnant woman knows to push when giving birth without being directly told. It is her instinct, and she does it even without knowledge as to why.
Knowledge vs Information
Knowledge is different than information in that information does not have to be interpreted. Information is facts and direct knowledge that does not require justification or interpretation. The capital of Canada is Ottawa. This statement is information because it cannot be debated and does not require justification. Knowledge can be complex, debated, and changed by beliefs or values. Knowledge is accompanied by understanding; information is facts that do not have to be understood to be true. If I were to say “blood is sacred,” that would be the knowledge to a Jehovah’s Witness member, but it can be refuted by other religions; it is not information. However, if I were to say “blood has a pH between 7.35 and 7.45”, that is information since it is a fact.
AI Discrepancies
AI has a multitude of issues overall, but when using AI, some common issues include false information, fabricated statistics, biases, and dated usage. For instance, when using ChatGPT or AI writing software, there have been times when the AI will create fake sources or citations to argue a point. It will mislead the user and strengthen its argument with sources that are misconstrued or fabricated. This has also been the case for numbers and statistics. There have been times when an AI model will use false numbers to create a graph or study that will ideally fit with the argument it is trying to make. There are also biases within AI systems. If an AI system is created by Amazon, it will resist expressing negative information about Amazon or push the user to use Amazon in their daily life. Lastly, AI models can only be as relevant as the software they are using. When new trends, slang, or research emerge, AI can only keep up if the software running is also up to date. Therefore, some AI models may be dated and use research or facts from years ago when current research has already proven that theory wrong.
The patterns within AI discrepancies all relate to the speed at which AI should work. False information and statistics are fabricated because it is quicker to create false information than to review thousands of articles to pick the ones best suited to the argument. AI can only be so modern because it takes time to update models with newer trends and information than it is to rely on older information that the models are already aware exist. And this is an issue among many AI models; writing software like ChatGPT, self-driving cars and road closures or construction, and AI social media with current events.

title: Association Rules
text:
Association Rules
An association rule identifies relationships between items in a dataset and can predict the related existence and behaviors in a data set. An association rule starts in the form of an expression X to Y, relating one occurrence to another. Sometimes association rules can be hidden and hard to mine. Data sources are large, have noise, and can have missing values if they are raw, but association rules make it easier to break down the data to understand patterns and correlations. There is also the challenge of finding an association that is interesting enough to work with and fits with the data. There are two important qualities for an association: support and confidence. Confidence is the reliability of an interaction found by calculating the probability of one item influencing another. Support is how frequently a combination of items appear together relative to the number of interactions they have.
Data Mining and Unsupervised Learning
Data mining is the broad field that focuses on pattern and relationship discovery with large databases. Some parts of data mining can include association rule mining, classification, or clustering. Whereas unsupervised learning is a sub-type of machine learning that focuses on discovering hidden structures in the data. Unsupervised learning can also include clustering and association rule mining since data mining is more of an umbrella term and includes unsupervised learning. Compared to supervised learning, which is where the machine learns from already coded and labelled databases, unsupervised learning can discover hidden connections without labels or variables that were inputted.
Zhang, S., & Wu, X. (2011). Fundamentals of association rules in data mining and knowledge discovery. Wiley Interdisciplinary Reviews: Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery, 1(2), 97-116.
Berry, M. W., Mohamed, A., & Yap, B. W. (Eds.). (2020). Supervised and unsupervised learning for data science (Vol. 10, pp. 978-3). Cham, Switzerland: Springer.

title: Machine Learning Pipeline
text:
A machine learning pipeline is a sequence of steps that make sure data is correctly prepared, models are trained, and results are evaluated properly. When starting my first machine learning pipeline, I used the National Assessment of Education Progress (NAEP) database since that is the database we are using for our project. It is also a state-level education dataset that works well with machine learning. The first step was to collect the data and determine what grade levels we would be looking at. We are looking at 4th and 8th grade level reading scores. The next step is to clean the data and rename or delete missing values and label the categories. These are some of the first steps when starting the machine learning pipeline.
Error Analysis
Accuracy is classified as a single number and can be identified through the number of correct predictions. What accuracy does not tell us is where the model made mistakes and the patterns in if certain groups were affected differently. Error analysis reveals these mistakes, biases in groups, weaknesses in the learning process, and areas where the model did not pick up patterns or relationships.
Overfitting is where the model understands the data too much and makes false connections. As well as capturing noise instead of patterns and does not know how to work with new data because it is too familiar with one dataset. Overfitting often mistakes noise for knowledge. Knowledge is where true patterns are shown in the data and shows the real structure of the data. Noise is the random errors or fluctuations that can happen in a dataset. Overfitting picks up on these fluctuations and mistakes them for actual patterns. For instance, since there is no personal student information in the pipeline, each student would have been assigned a number. Overfitting would take the noise, the participant’s number, and create connections to the data that has no real meaning. This creates a fragile and irrelevant relationship with the data that is not of any use for finding patterns and connections. If we were finding knowledge in the dataset, we would see connections between reading scores being lower with students on government assistance, or reading scores being higher in higher-income areas.

title: Text Mining Patterns
text:
Text mining can reveal patterns for hidden statistical relationships, large patterns across multiple articles and readings, patterns over time, and on a large scale. Text mining differs from close reading because it operates on a larger scale. However, text mining also comes with disadvantages, including misinterpretations, false patterns, a lack of understanding of the full meaning, overfitting, and biases. Text mining is helpful when looking at trends over time, co-occurring patterns, cultural trends, and showing relationships between people and ideas. The disadvantages come from AI models not being able to notice sarcasm, tone, or trending references of slang. As well as suggesting patterns where they don’t exist, believing something is important because it is written often, overtraining, making the AI familiar with only a few specific text forms, and fragmenting the texts, which takes away the author’s voice.
NLP and LLMs can strengthen my research project by helping extract, structure, and interpret datasets, mainly because they are large and structured. Since I am using NAEP reading scores and socioeconomic data, LLMs can help summarize the large amount of data we are getting from the US Census and the NAEP. LLMs can also help standardize the datasets and create meaningful relationships within the data. For example. State language is often inconsistent; they record low-income differently or “disadvantaged youth”, but do not list out how they vary from state to state. This will help us improve our models and reduce the time it takes to process the data. NLP can help with our research by analyzing patterns. For instance, topic modeling, which can identify themes such as remote learning. This helps to add depth to the data beyond numbers and helps quantify qualitative data. NLP can also help make structured variables from unstructured data. Oftentimes, state reports mention “learning loss” or “digital divide,” which may be tied to socioeconomic hardships within the student population, but we want NLP to look at these reports and create new explanatory variables that we can use with the other data.
Some limitations when using NLP and LLMs include data bias, hallucinations, lack of true casual reasoning, data privacy, and inconsistency. LLMs are trained on existing data and have historical biases. This can skew results by overstating socioeconomic effects or underrepresenting marginalized groups. The solution would be to look at the datasets and cross-reference the validity, as well as use multiple data sources to fact check. Hallucinations are also a limitation of using the text mining models since they can fabricate statistics or report incorrect claims about causal relationships. The solution is to use LLMs and NLP as a tool instead of being reliant on it for facts. Additionally, LLMs can lack true casual reasoning because they sometimes identify patterns that don’t have causation. For instance, an LLM might suggest that the pandemic was the main cause of the decline in reading scores without taking into account socioeconomic factors that affect those scores. Using gradient boosting models, control variables, and pre and post pandemic scores would help mitigate the issue with lacking casual reasoning. Data privacy is also an issue with our research because we are working with reading scores and demographics. Focusing on state-level rather than individual data will help solve this problem. Lastly, inconsistency is common with LLMs because they can produce somewhat different answers for the same question. We would solve this problem by using the same prompt when working with AI models. Overall, LLMs and NLP can be powerful tools, but they need to be monitored and controlled to achieve the highest productivity in our research.
AI for Research Guide:
Brainstorming and Planning…
Give AI a clear role
Use consistent prompts
Only use AI as a tool, not a reliance
Use AI for planning and checking only, not for final conclusions
Ethics…
Monitor for biases and overtraining
Never use personal information in AI models
Use anonymous data only
Fact check information and research citations
Technical Concerns
Monitor for hallucinations
Fact check information and research citations
Cross check AI with the datasets
Use proper regression and experiments to distinguish between correlation and causation
Document prompts, steps and code for reproducibility
Academic Concerns…
Use AI only as a tool not a final product
Rewrite main points and outputs
Cite all sources
Make your argument original

title: LLMs
text:
LLMs are good at recognizing patterns, structures, and language. They can explain text across multiple platforms, help brainstorm and create ideas, and recognize statistical relationships. LLMs tend to fail when you are looking for precision and long-term patterns. LLMs also have trouble when you are working with multi-step reasoning because they can create shortcuts or get lost in the process. When using LLMs, I have mainly encountered hallucinations where fake but plausible information is created. For instance, I have seen LLMs draw conclusions based on statements that are frequently written. An LLM has written that remote learning has widened the learning gaps without providing evidence to support this claim, as it is often cited in other texts.
In the next 5 years, AI will most likely not have a huge effect on psychology because it is a discipline that relies on emotion and human experience, and AI has a hard time replicating those areas; unlike programming and marketing, which mainly use numbers and computers to make predictions and solve problems. For instance, therapists use tone, past experiences, empathy, and human experience to relate to their clients and perform their job. Research that uses numbers like statistical psychology and meta-analysis will change the most because it uses previously published research to make predictions, and LLMs and machine learning will be able to analyze these previously published texts. Whereas current research and person-based jobs like social workers, therapists, and psychologists will mostly remain unchanged because of their use of emotions and empathy.
In statistical psychology and research, text analysis and data management will change the most because AI will be able to filter through thousands of articles in record time, making it significantly easier to write manuscripts and publish papers. Data management will change because it will be easier to generate longitudinal studies and make predictions about trends in research. Data collection will roughly stay the same because it still requires human participation. Imaging data will still need to be collected by humans and cannot be automated because of the level of specificity required. MRIs need to be closely monitored in case of an emergency, and PET scans require a trained professional to administer. Overall, after the initial data is collected, AI will play a part in analyzing and publishing works.
